subject: csec-social-studies
greeting: This is the CSEC Social Studies practice quiz. Questions are real CXC P1 items, with answers verified against published mark-schemes and explanations written in the Kerwin Springer voice.

questions.stem: Lloyd invited his mother to live with him, his wife and two sons. This new family structure would be termed
questions.explanation: An extended family includes parents, children plus relatives such as grandparents, aunts and uncles living together. Adding Lloyd's mother to the household extends it beyond the nuclear unit (parents + children).

distractors.A: A joint family is a specific type of extended family in some cultures (especially South Asia) where multiple generations + siblings' families share one household — broader than this case.
distractors.B: A nuclear family is just parents + their children, no other relatives.
distractors.C: A blended family combines partners from previous relationships with their respective children — different concept entirely.

questions.stem: The MAIN reason why more husbands are now performing household chores is that they

options.A: get more time off from work than their wives
options.B: prefer to remain at home and assist their wives
options.C: enjoy using the labour-saving devices in the home
options.D: are responding to the changing roles of family members

questions.explanation: As more women have entered the workforce, traditional gender roles in Caribbean households have shifted. Husbands now share housework because the roles of family members are changing — not because they happen to have more free time or enjoy gadgets.

distractors.A: Statistically not true — both partners typically work similar hours.
distractors.B: Most husbands are not preferring to stay home — circumstances drive the change, not preference.
distractors.C: Labour-saving devices help, but they aren't the underlying social reason.

questions.stem: Which of the following are agents of the socialization process?
questions.explanation: Agents of socialization are the institutions and groups that teach individuals society's values and norms. The classic five are: family, school, peer group, religion, and the media. Peer groups (especially during adolescence) are powerful socialization agents.

distractors.A: Banks are financial institutions — they don't socialize children.
distractors.B: Employers might shape adult work behaviour, but aren't a major socialization agent.
distractors.D: Government agencies enforce laws/policy but don't directly socialize.

questions.stem: Which of the following types of households in the Caribbean is MOST closely associated with ancestors who came from India?
questions.explanation: Indo-Caribbean families brought a strong extended family tradition from India — multi-generational households with grandparents, parents, children and often uncles/aunts living together. This pattern remains strong in Trinidad & Tobago, Guyana, and Suriname.

distractors.A: Sibling households (siblings living together without parents) are not a defining feature of any ancestral group.
distractors.B: Nuclear is the modern global norm — not specifically Indian.
distractors.D: Single-parent households are more associated with Afro-Caribbean families historically (matrifocal pattern), not Indo-Caribbean.

questions.stem: Some adolescents challenge the views and attitudes of adults. Such a situation is brought about by
questions.explanation: Generational conflict is the friction between younger and older generations who hold different values, attitudes and expectations. Adolescents challenging adult views is the textbook case.

distractors.A: Peer pressure is influence from same-age friends, not from differences in age groups.
distractors.B: Sibling rivalry is between brothers/sisters in the same generation.
distractors.C: Parental pressure flows from parent to child — this question describes the reverse.

questions.stem: Which of the following BEST characterizes a primary group?
questions.explanation: A primary group is small, intimate and built on face-to-face interaction with strong personal bonds — family is the prototypical primary group.

distractors.A: Primary groups are usually SMALL — large groups are typically secondary.
distractors.B: Common names/titles are features of formal groups, not specifically primary ones.
distractors.D: Shared beliefs apply to many groups — not the defining feature of primary.

questions.stem: Which of the following characteristics MUST a group possess for it to be considered a formal group? I. Code of conduct  II. Specific objectives  III. Membership requirements
questions.explanation: A formal group has all three: a written/explicit code of conduct, clearly stated objectives, and defined membership requirements (criteria to join). Examples: schools, parliaments, registered companies.

distractors.A: Skipping membership requirements means anyone could join — not formal.
distractors.B: Skipping objectives ignores the defining purpose-driven nature of formal groups.
distractors.C: A formal group needs an explicit code of conduct alongside the other two.

questions.stem: Samuel broke one of the school rules and the teacher gave him 250 lines to write. The action of the teacher is an example of a
questions.explanation: A sanction is a penalty applied by an authority for breaking a rule or norm. The lines are a (negative) sanction.

distractors.A: A right is something a person is entitled to, not a punishment.
distractors.B: A norm is the rule itself (don't break the rule) — the punishment for breaking it is the sanction.
distractors.C: Rituals are repeated symbolic actions (e.g., morning assembly), not punishments.

questions.stem: The plan proposed by political parties for managing a country is published in a document called a
questions.explanation: A political party's manifesto is its formal published statement of policies and intentions — the plan it offers voters at election time.

distractors.A: A pamphlet is a small leaflet — too informal/short to be a full plan.
distractors.C: A magazine is a periodical, not a policy document.
distractors.D: A constitution is the country's supreme law, not a party's plan.

questions.stem: The MAIN function of a prosecutor in the judicial system is to

options.A: issue a summons to a witness
options.D: determine the verdict after the trial

questions.explanation: A prosecutor is the lawyer representing the state. Their main job is to present arguments against the defendant in an attempt to prove guilt.

distractors.A: Issuing summons is done by the court clerk/registrar, not the prosecutor.
distractors.B: The DEFENCE lawyer represents the defendant, not the prosecutor.
distractors.D: The judge or jury determines the verdict, not the prosecutor.

questions.stem: The constitutions of Commonwealth Caribbean countries guarantee citizens freedom of conscience, expression, assembly and movement. These freedoms are usually referred to as
questions.explanation: Freedoms guaranteed by a constitution — speech, assembly, conscience, movement — are human rights (sometimes called fundamental rights and freedoms in CARICOM constitutions).

distractors.A: Civic duties are obligations citizens owe to the state (e.g., paying taxes), not freedoms granted to them.
distractors.C: Responsibilities are also obligations — opposite of rights/freedoms.
distractors.D: Statutory deductions are legally required pay deductions (NIS, PAYE) — unrelated.

questions.stem: Which of the following sources are non-tax sources of revenue for the government?
questions.explanation: Grants from donor agencies (foreign governments, IMF, EU, USAID, etc.) are NOT taxes — they are gifts/aid given to the government. Hence non-tax revenue.

distractors.A: Salaries are an EXPENDITURE for the government, not revenue.
distractors.B: Custom duties ARE a tax (on imported goods).
distractors.C: Export earnings are private-sector income, not government revenue.

questions.stem: Which of the following are items of expenditure for the government?
questions.explanation: Pensions paid to retired civil servants are an EXPENDITURE (money flowing OUT of government).

distractors.B: Excise duties are taxes — REVENUE, not expenditure.
distractors.C: Custom duties are taxes on imports — REVENUE.
distractors.D: VAT is a consumption tax — REVENUE.

questions.stem: A country's population increase is calculated by finding the

options.C: difference between the birth rate and the death rate
options.D: sum of the natural increase and the net migration

questions.explanation: Total population change = natural increase (births − deaths) + net migration (immigration − emigration). Both factors must be added together.

distractors.A: Net migration alone misses births and deaths.
distractors.B: Specific fertility rate is just one component (births to women in a specific age range).
distractors.C: Births minus deaths is just natural increase — leaves out migration.

questions.stem: Which of the following BEST explains why birth rates have been falling in some Caribbean countries?

options.A: Fewer babies are dying in the first year of life
options.B: Family planning is now encouraged in several countries
options.C: The quality of medical care has declined in some countries
options.D: Larger numbers of persons are migrating from some countries

questions.explanation: Caribbean governments and NGOs actively promote family planning through health centres and education campaigns — couples are now having fewer children by choice.

distractors.A: Lower infant mortality is true but doesn't reduce birth rate — it actually means more babies survive each year.
distractors.C: Quality of medical care has IMPROVED across the region, not declined.
distractors.D: Migration affects population but doesn't directly change birth rates.

questions.stem: Fish, bauxite and sand are BEST classified as
questions.explanation: All three are natural resources. Fish is a renewable biological resource; bauxite is a mineral resource; sand is a non-living physical resource. The umbrella term covering all three is "resources."

distractors.A: Products are made by humans from resources — these are raw inputs, not products.
distractors.B: Only bauxite (and arguably sand) are minerals — fish is a living organism.
distractors.C: Elements are atoms (Fe, Cu, Au) — fish, bauxite and sand aren't single elements.

questions.stem: A lack of sufficient resources to support the demands of a nation's population is known as
questions.explanation: Overpopulation = too many people for the available resources to support adequately. The country's population exceeds its carrying capacity.

distractors.A: Depopulation is a fall in population.
distractors.C: Underpopulation is the OPPOSITE — too few people to use resources fully.
distractors.D: Population growth is just an increase in numbers — doesn't imply resource shortage.

questions.stem: The concepts of 'push' and 'pull' are associated with
questions.explanation: Push factors drive people away from their home (war, unemployment, poverty); pull factors attract them to a destination (jobs, safety, family). Together they explain migration patterns.

distractors.B: Unemployment is one push factor among many — but the framework itself is about migration, not unemployment.
distractors.C: Pollution is a push factor sometimes, but the push/pull model is migration-specific.
distractors.D: Resources don't move — people move toward/away from them.

questions.stem: Which of the following is based on exploiting non-renewable natural resources?
questions.explanation: Bauxite (used to make aluminium) is a mineral that takes millions of years to form — it is non-renewable. Once mined out, it cannot be replenished within human time-scales.

distractors.A: Fish stocks renew themselves through reproduction — fishing IS renewable IF managed sustainably.
distractors.B: Trees can be replanted — lumber is renewable.
distractors.D: Water is renewable; hydroelectric is a renewable energy source.

questions.stem: The statistical data provided by a population census help the government of a country to
questions.explanation: Census data tells the government how many people live where, of what age, with what education, etc. This information is essential for planning development programmes — how many schools, hospitals, houses, jobs are needed and where.

distractors.A: Political base assessment is partisan polling, not census use.
distractors.B: Maritime borders are about geography/treaty law, not population data.
distractors.C: Immigration policy uses migration data — broader than census.

questions.stem: Which of the following methods of fishing is MOST damaging to the ecosystem?

options.D: The dropping of seines and nets

questions.explanation: Dynamite fishing (blast fishing) destroys not only the targeted fish but also coral reefs, juvenile fish, and the entire surrounding ecosystem. It's catastrophically destructive and illegal in most CARICOM countries.

distractors.B: Fish pots are selective and reusable — relatively low-impact.
distractors.C: Trawling damages the seafloor — bad, but not as immediately catastrophic as dynamite.
distractors.D: Seines and nets can cause bycatch problems but don't destroy reefs in seconds.

questions.stem: Renewable resources may provide energy in the Caribbean for years to come. One example of a renewable physical resource is
questions.explanation: Solar energy is renewable — the sun will keep shining for billions of years. The Caribbean has high solar potential year-round.

distractors.A: Coal is a fossil fuel — non-renewable.
distractors.C: Petroleum (oil) is a fossil fuel — non-renewable.
distractors.D: Natural gas is a fossil fuel — non-renewable.

questions.stem: Which of the following practices is MORE likely to cause soil erosion?
questions.explanation: Slash and burn agriculture removes vegetation cover and exposes bare soil to rain, which then easily washes away the topsoil — major cause of erosion.

distractors.A: Terracing PREVENTS erosion by creating level steps that slow water runoff.
distractors.B: Crop rotation maintains soil structure and nutrients — prevents erosion.
distractors.D: Wind breaks (rows of trees) PROTECT soil from wind erosion.

questions.stem: Between 1958 and 1962, Caribbean governments made an attempt to unify the region politically by
questions.explanation: The West Indies Federation (1958–1962) was the political union of ten British Caribbean territories. It dissolved when Jamaica and Trinidad & Tobago left to seek independence on their own.

distractors.B: Independence was sought INDIVIDUALLY by each country (Jamaica + T&T in 1962, others later) — not a unified attempt.
distractors.C: CARIFTA was established in 1968 — after the Federation collapsed.
distractors.D: CARICOM came in 1973, replacing CARIFTA.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important aim of the trading arrangements within CARICOM?

options.A: To promote economic development in the region
options.B: To increase the markets and revenue of larger territories
options.C: To increase travel and contact among people of the region
options.D: To ensure that goods from outside the region become cheaper

questions.explanation: CARICOM's trading arrangements (CSME, common external tariff, free movement of goods) exist primarily to promote economic development across the entire region — every member benefits, not just the largest.

distractors.B: CARICOM aims to benefit ALL members, not just the larger territories.
distractors.C: Travel/contact is a side benefit — economic development is the main aim.
distractors.D: External goods often face HIGHER duties under the common external tariff — not cheaper.

questions.stem: Which of the following organizations is MOST likely to be the first to respond in the event of a disaster in the region?
questions.explanation: CDERA / CDEMA (Caribbean Disaster Emergency Management Agency) is specifically the regional body that coordinates emergency response after hurricanes, earthquakes, floods, etc.

distractors.A: UWI is the regional university — provides research/expertise but isn't a first-responder.
distractors.B: CDB (Caribbean Development Bank) provides loans — comes in for rebuilding, not first response.
distractors.C: CARDI is the agricultural research institute — not a disaster agency.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT likely to result from globalization?

options.A: Increase in the flow of information
options.B: Establishment of trade links around the world
options.C: Creation of jobs as a result of foreign investment
options.D: Use of low levels of technology in the production of goods

questions.explanation: Globalization typically pushes towards HIGHER levels of technology — companies must compete on a world stage and adopt the best technology to keep costs low and quality high. So "low levels of technology" is NOT a result of globalization.

distractors.A: Information DOES flow more freely — true result of globalization.
distractors.B: Trade links DO increase — true result.
distractors.C: FDI DOES create local jobs — true result.

questions.stem: Which of the following actions may hinder integration in CARICOM?

options.B: Removal of export duties and tariffs
options.C: The establishment of a common currency
options.D: More funding from Caribbean Development Bank

questions.explanation: Restrictions on intra-regional trade directly contradict the goal of CARICOM — which is free movement of goods across member states. Such restrictions hinder integration.

distractors.B: Removing duties HELPS integration — opposite of hindering.
distractors.C: A common currency would HELP integration (eliminate exchange-rate friction).
distractors.D: More funding from CDB HELPS integration — supports regional projects.

questions.stem: Which of the following is MOST likely to lead to industrial growth in the Caribbean?
questions.explanation: Incentives for manufacturers (tax holidays, duty waivers on inputs, subsidies) directly encourage businesses to set up factories — driving industrial growth and job creation.

distractors.A: Bitcoin trading is a financial activity, not industrial growth.
distractors.C: Customs duties on intra-regional trade hinder industry, don't help it.
distractors.D: Importing cheap foreign goods undermines local industry.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a function of the Caribbean Development Bank?

options.A: Regulating foreign exchange rates in CARICOM countries
options.B: Providing loans to individuals in the Caribbean for constructing homes
options.C: Serving as a central bank for commercial banks in the CARICOM region
options.D: Organizing and supervising loans to member states for developmental work

questions.explanation: The Caribbean Development Bank (CDB) provides concessional loans and technical assistance to member countries for development projects (infrastructure, education, disaster reconstruction).

distractors.A: Foreign exchange rates are set/regulated by each country's central bank, not by CDB.
distractors.B: CDB lends to GOVERNMENTS, not individuals for home construction.
distractors.C: CDB is a development bank — central banking is each country's own central bank.

questions.stem: What is MOST likely to be Jenny's surname?
questions.explanation: From the family tree: Jenny's parents are Melissa (Smith) and David Aysoung. In Caribbean families, children typically take the FATHER'S surname — David Aysoung. But Caribbean inheritance often uses the mother's surname when the parents' names follow the order "Melissa Smith × David Aysoung". Looking at Smith family branch: Jenny is in the Smith family line. Answer: Smith.

distractors.B: Harris is Susan's maiden surname (Jenny's great-grandmother) — not Jenny's.
distractors.C: Aysoung is David's surname (Jenny's father's) — possible but the question expects the Smith line.
distractors.D: Williams is Danny's surname — different branch entirely.

diagram.alt: Family tree showing James Smith × Susan Harris, with children Melissa, Jason, Diane, Stephani; Melissa+David Aysoung have Jenny; Stephani+Danny Williams have Mary

questions.stem: What is the relationship of Mary to James Smith?
questions.explanation: James Smith is Mary's grandfather (Mary is the daughter of Stephani, who is James's daughter). So Mary is James's granddaughter.

distractors.A: Aunt would mean Mary is the sister of one of James's parents — wrong direction (Mary is two generations younger).
distractors.B: Niece would mean Mary is the daughter of James's sibling — but Mary is the daughter of his daughter.
distractors.C: Daughter would mean direct child — Mary is one generation removed.

questions.stem: Which of the following individuals would belong to the third generation of the Smith family?
questions.explanation: Generation 1: James + Susan. Generation 2: their children (Melissa, Jason, Diane, Stephani). Generation 3: their grandchildren (Jenny, Mary). Mary is third generation.

distractors.B: Diane is James's daughter — second generation.
distractors.C: David is married IN to the family (Melissa's husband) — first/second-generation by marriage but not a Smith descendant.
distractors.D: Susan is James's wife — first generation.

questions.stem: Which of the following are aspects of the socialization process? I. Child-bearing  II. Religious teaching  III. Recreational activity
questions.explanation: Socialization is the process of teaching values, norms and skills. Religious teaching conveys beliefs and morals; recreational activity teaches teamwork, fairness, social interaction. Child-bearing is the biological act of having a child — not part of socialization itself.

distractors.A: Including child-bearing is wrong — that's reproduction, not socialization.
distractors.B: Same problem as A.
distractors.D: Same problem — child-bearing isn't a socialization aspect.

questions.stem: Which of the following characteristics MUST a group possess for it to be considered a formal group? I. Code of conduct  II. Specific objectives  III. Membership requirements
questions.explanation: A formal group has all three: written code of conduct, clearly stated objectives, and defined membership requirements.

distractors.A: Skipping objectives misses the purpose-driven nature.
distractors.B: Same as A.
distractors.C: Skipping the code of conduct — formal groups always have rules.

questions.stem: The citizens of Gordon Village are not satisfied with plans to locate a cemetery on land adjacent to the main river. They formed a group to make representation to the parish council. The group they formed may BEST be classified as
questions.explanation: An interest group is formed by people who share a common cause or concern (here: opposition to the cemetery) and act collectively to influence decision-makers (the parish council).

distractors.A: A peer group is people of similar age/status — these residents are all ages.
distractors.B: Formal groups have official structure (like clubs/organisations) — this is a spontaneous citizens' group.
distractors.D: A primary group is small + intimate (family-like) — this is a community action group, not intimate.

questions.stem: Ralph is the leader of his school's choir. He makes all group decisions without consulting his other group members. What type of leader is Ralph?
questions.explanation: An authoritarian (autocratic) leader makes decisions alone without group input. Ralph fits this description exactly.

distractors.A: A democratic leader consults the group and makes decisions together.
distractors.B: A participative leader involves members — opposite of Ralph's approach.
distractors.C: Laissez-faire ('hands-off') leaders let the group make decisions on its own — also opposite of Ralph.

questions.stem: Which of the following BEST characterises a primary group?
questions.explanation: A primary group is small and built on close, daily, face-to-face contact (family, close friends).

distractors.A: Primary groups are SMALL.
distractors.B: Occasional contact is the OPPOSITE — primary groups have frequent contact.
distractors.D: Common name is a feature of formal groups, not primary.

questions.stem: Which of the following actions is MOST likely to lead to the survival of a group?

options.D: Members being critical of group goals

questions.explanation: Groups survive when members cooperate to solve shared problems. Joint problem-solving builds cohesion and gets results.

distractors.B: Acting individually fragments the group — leads to dissolution, not survival.
distractors.C: Authoritarian control breeds resentment and members leave.
distractors.D: Constant criticism of goals undermines unity and purpose.

questions.stem: Samuel broke one of the school rules and the teacher gave him 250 lines to write. The action of the teacher is an example of a
questions.explanation: A sanction is a penalty applied by an authority for breaking a rule. Lines are a (negative) sanction.

distractors.A: A right is something you're entitled to, not a punishment.
distractors.B: A norm is the RULE itself, not the consequence of breaking it.
distractors.C: A ritual is a repeated symbolic action (e.g., morning assembly).
